agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Critical SQL injection vulnerability — unsanitized user input interpolated directly into query string"
  },
  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "SQL Injection via f-string query construction",
      "risk": "Full database compromise: an attacker can read, modify, or delete any data, bypass authentication, or execute administrative operations via crafted customer_id or status_filter values",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — query construction on lines 2-6",
      "reproduction": "GET /api/deliveries?customer_id=' OR 1=1; DROP TABLE deliveries; --&status_filter=shipped — the f-string interpolates the payload directly into the SQL, producing: SELECT * FROM deliveries WHERE customer_id = '' OR 1=1; DROP TABLE deliveries; --' AND status = 'shipped'",
      "fix": "Use parameterized queries: db_conn.execute(\"SELECT * FROM deliveries WHERE customer_id = ? AND status = ?\", (customer_id, status_filter)). Never interpolate user input into SQL strings.",
      "time_to_fix": "5 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Security, data integrity, availability"
    },
    {
      "title": "SELECT * exposes all columns to public API consumers",
      "risk": "Leaks internal fields (e.g. internal_notes, cost_price, driver_ssn, or any future sensitive column) to unauthenticated callers. Schema changes silently expand the attack surface.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — SELECT *",
      "reproduction": "Add a sensitive column to the deliveries table; it is immediately returned to every API caller without code changes.",
      "fix": "Enumerate only the columns the API contract requires: SELECT id, customer_id, status, tracking_number, created_at FROM deliveries ...",
      "time_to_fix": "10 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Security, data minimization"
    },
    {
      "title": "No authorization boundary — any customer can query any other customer's deliveries",
      "risk": "Horizontal privilege escalation: a logged-in customer can supply another customer's ID and retrieve their delivery history.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — customer_id taken directly from request params",
      "reproduction": "Authenticate as customer A, call GET /api/deliveries?customer_id=<customer_B_id>&status_filter=shipped — returns customer B's deliveries.",
      "fix": "Derive customer_id from the authenticated session/token, not from query parameters. If an admin endpoint needs to query by arbitrary customer, gate it behind an authorization check.",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Authorization, data privacy"
    }
  ],
  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No input validation on status_filter",
      "description": "status_filter is not validated against a known enum of delivery statuses. Even after parameterization, unconstrained values will return empty results silently instead of a 400 error, making debugging harder and allowing enumeration probes.",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — status_filter parameter"
    },
    {
      "title": "No pagination or result-set limit",
      "description": "A query with no LIMIT clause on a public endpoint allows a single request to dump the entire table (especially after SQL injection flattens the WHERE clause). Even legitimately, a high-volume customer could trigger an OOM or timeout.",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — query has no LIMIT/OFFSET"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Add logging/audit trail for delivery lookups to detect enumeration or exfiltration attempts",
    "Add an index on (customer_id, status) if one does not already exist — this is the exact query pattern",
    "Return a well-typed DTO/dataclass instead of raw row tuples to decouple the DB schema from the API response"
  ],
  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "This code is exploitable the moment it receives traffic. Any deployment exposes the full database to unauthenticated SQL injection.",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback is safe but does not remediate data already exfiltrated or destroyed during exposure window.",
    "backward_compatibility": "Parameterized queries and explicit column lists are backward-compatible changes to callers.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No mention of query logging, error handling, or alerting. A successful injection would leave no application-level trace.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": true,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": ["SQL injection allows arbitrary DB mutations including data destruction and schema changes"]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Do not deploy. If already deployed, take the endpoint offline immediately, rotate database credentials, and audit query logs for injection attempts."
  },
  "escalations": [],
  "confidence": {
    "level": 99,
    "high_confidence_areas": ["SQL injection is unambiguous and trivially exploitable", "Authorization gap is clear from the function signature and caller description"],
    "low_confidence_areas": ["Exact column names to whitelist in SELECT depend on the schema, which was not provided"],
    "assumptions": ["db_conn.execute supports parameterized queries (standard for Python DB-API 2.0)", "No WAF or ORM layer sits between the HTTP handler and this function"]
  }
}

content.thinking: SQL injection vulnerability, clear as day.
